<findings>

[observation] [info] High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
[observation] [info] Cohesion of 1.0 and coupling of 0 confirm this zone is correctly scoped: it owns orchestration concerns and nothing else leaks in or out.
[observation] [info] No test files are detected in this zone; orchestration scripts that spawn subprocesses should have at least smoke-level integration tests to catch CLI contract regressions.
[observation] [info] The presence of config.js alongside cli.js and web.js reflects the unified config command pattern described in CLAUDE.md — all three are peer entry points, not nested utilities.
[pattern] [warning] Orchestration scripts communicate exclusively via subprocess spawn, not imports — this means CLI contract regressions (renamed flags, changed exit codes) are undetectable by static import-graph analysis and require dedicated CLI contract tests to enforce.
[anti-pattern] [warning] Config reading is implemented independently in each domain package (rex reads .rex/config.json, hench reads .hench/config.json, sourcevision reads .sourcevision/) while config.js at the orchestration layer provides a unified view. There is no shared config-reader utility in the Foundation tier, so format changes must be patched in multiple independent implementations.
[suggestion] [info] The zone mixes slow-changing metadata files (.gitignore, .npmrc, CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md) with frequently-modified entry points (cli.js, web.js, config.js). Consider adding a zone-pin subdividing this zone into 'monorepo-root-config' (metadata) and 'monorepo-entry-points' (scripts) to expose change-rate differences and reduce blast-radius confusion during entry-point refactors.
[suggestion] [warning] config.js has an implicit schema dependency on domain config file formats that is undetectable by import-graph analysis and unprotected by the gateway pattern. Document the config file schemas as stable interfaces in PACKAGE_GUIDELINES.md and add a schema-validation test in the CI pipeline to catch format drift before it silently breaks config.js.

</findings>

<insights>

- High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
- The orchestration layer correctly delegates to CLIs via spawn rather than importing package internals, preserving the four-tier dependency hierarchy.
- Contains both developer-facing documentation (CLAUDE.md, README.md, PACKAGE_GUIDELINES.md) and executable scripts (ci.js, cli.js, web.js), which is appropriate for a monorepo root.
- Zero coupling to any downstream package is a strong architectural signal that this boundary is correctly drawn.
- Cohesion of 1.0 and coupling of 0 confirm this zone is correctly scoped: it owns orchestration concerns and nothing else leaks in or out.
- The presence of config.js alongside cli.js and web.js reflects the unified config command pattern described in CLAUDE.md — all three are peer entry points, not nested utilities.
- No test files are detected in this zone; orchestration scripts that spawn subprocesses should have at least smoke-level integration tests to catch CLI contract regressions.
- Subprocess-spawn architecture creates a static-analysis blind spot: CLI contracts between orchestration scripts and downstream packages are invisible to import-graph tooling, so architectural drift in CLI interfaces cannot be detected by cohesion/coupling metrics alone.
- Orchestration scripts communicate exclusively via subprocess spawn, not imports — this means CLI contract regressions (renamed flags, changed exit codes) are undetectable by static import-graph analysis and require dedicated CLI contract tests to enforce.
- config.js at the orchestration layer is the single entry point for unified config reads, but domain packages (rex, hench, sourcevision) each independently implement their own config loading (e.g. .rex/config.json, .hench/config.json). This means config-reading logic is duplicated across tiers rather than centralised — a violation of the DRY principle that will cause drift if config file formats evolve.
- Config reading is implemented independently in each domain package (rex reads .rex/config.json, hench reads .hench/config.json, sourcevision reads .sourcevision/) while config.js at the orchestration layer provides a unified view. There is no shared config-reader utility in the Foundation tier, so format changes must be patched in multiple independent implementations.
- config.js at the orchestration layer reads domain config files (.rex/config.json, .hench/config.json, .sourcevision/) directly via filesystem reads rather than via domain package APIs. Since orchestration is defined as a spawn-only layer with no library imports, this file-reading is the correct mechanism — but it means the orchestration layer has an implicit schema dependency on domain config file formats that is entirely invisible to both import-graph analysis and the gateway pattern. Config format changes in domain packages will silently break config.js with no static detection.
- The 29-file zone includes both version-controlled metadata files (.gitignore, .npmrc, CODEX.md) and executable entry points (cli.js, web.js, ci.js). These two file classes have entirely different change rates and owners — metadata files change rarely and by convention, entry points change frequently during development. Grouping them in one zone obscures change-rate patterns that would otherwise signal refactoring pressure on the entry points.
- config.js has an implicit schema dependency on domain config file formats that is undetectable by import-graph analysis and unprotected by the gateway pattern. Document the config file schemas as stable interfaces in PACKAGE_GUIDELINES.md and add a schema-validation test in the CI pipeline to catch format drift before it silently breaks config.js.
- The zone mixes slow-changing metadata files (.gitignore, .npmrc, CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md) with frequently-modified entry points (cli.js, web.js, config.js). Consider adding a zone-pin subdividing this zone into 'monorepo-root-config' (metadata) and 'monorepo-entry-points' (scripts) to expose change-rate differences and reduce blast-radius confusion during entry-point refactors.
- [call graph] 449 internal calls, 0 outgoing, 0 incoming (cohesion: 1, coupling: 0)

</insights>
